text: This is a video demonstration of robotic lateral pelvic node dissection in rectal cancer using DaVinci XI platform. We refer to this schematic representation of cross-section of pelvis. The lateral pelvic node area for rectal cancer lies on either side of internal iliac vasculature beyond the pelvic hypogastric plexus which forms the medial most margin.

This is a video demonstration of robotic lateral pelvic node dissection in rectal cancer using DaVinci XI platform. We refer to this schematic representation of cross-section of pelvis. The lateral pelvic node area for rectal cancer lies on either side of internal iliac vasculature beyond the pelvic hypogastric plexus which forms the medial most margin.

The surgeon inserts ports along a line four centimeters above and parallel to the line joining the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The surgical field is cleared lateral to the internal iliac vasculature, bounded laterally by the external iliac vein and obturator internus muscle, and medially by branches of the internal iliac vessels.

Robotic ports are inserted along a line four centimeters above and parallel to the line joining the right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus. The surgeon begins clearing the area lateral to the internal iliac vasculature, bounded laterally by the external iliac vein and obturator internus muscle, and medially by branches of the internal iliac vessels.

The surgeon completes posterior rectal mobilization in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, clearly defining the pelvic hypogastric plexus from the medial side. Lateral peritoneal cuts are made along the line of Toll and extended over the external iliac vessels, adequately exposing the lateral pelvic node-bearing area.

The surgeon completes posterior rectal mobilization in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, clearly defining the pelvic hypogastric plexus from the medial side. Lateral peritoneal cuts are made along the line of Toll and extended over the external iliac vessels, adequately exposing the lateral pelvic node-bearing area.
The surgeon identifies and reflects the ureter, creating the barovar cycle space to provide adequate working space for the lateral pelvic node dissection.
The surgeon identifies and reflects the ureter, followed by the creation of the barovar cycle space, providing adequate working space for the lateral pelvic node dissection.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery and traces it back to its origin at the internal iliac artery, providing a clear visual reference for locating the internal iliac artery during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery and traces it to its origin at the internal iliac artery, providing a clear visual reference for locating the internal iliac artery during the dissection.

The surgeon dissects and reflects the fibrofatty tissue medially along the external iliac vessels, exposing the external iliac vein. As the tissue is further reflected, the obturator internus muscle becomes fully visible.

The surgeon dissects and reflects the fibrofatty tissue medially along the course of the external iliac vessels. As the tissue is reflected off the external iliac vein, the obturator internus muscle becomes visible, fully exposed by the continued medial reflection of the fibrofatty tissue.

The surgeon performs further medial mobilization to carefully identify and expose the obturator nerve. The nodal tissue is meticulously separated from the internal iliac artery and the obliterated umbilical artery.

The surgeon performs further medial mobilization to carefully identify and expose the obturator nerve. The nodal tissue is separated from the internal iliac artery and the obliterated umbilical artery, revealing these structures clearly.
The surgeon dissects towards the common iliac bifurcation, separating nodal tissue from the surrounding perivascular fat and reflecting it off the obturator nerve.
The surgeon dissects towards the common iliac bifurcation, separating nodal tissue from the surrounding perivascular fat and reflecting it off the obturator nerve.
The obturator vessels are preserved as the boundaries of the lateral zone dissection become clearly visible.
The surgeon demonstrates the lateral zone dissection, with clearly visible boundaries, while preserving the obturator vessels.
The medial zone is visualized, bounded laterally by branches of the internal iliac artery, medially by the pelvic hypogastric plexus, and posteriorly by the sacrum.

The medial zone is visualized, bounded laterally by branches of the internal iliac artery, medially by the pelvic hypogastric plexus, and posteriorly by the sacrum, with no active dissection occurring in this area.

The surgeon creates a medial space between the pelvic hypogastric plexus and the branches of the internal iliac artery. The node-bearing fibrofatty tissue along the branches of the internal iliac artery is meticulously cleared, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.

The surgeon creates a medial space between the pelvic hypogastric plexus and the branches of the internal iliac artery. The node-bearing fibrofatty tissue in this area is then meticulously cleared along the branches of the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon identifies the distal limit of the dissection at the first branch of the inferior vesicular artery, marking the boundary of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the distal limit of the dissection at the first branch of the inferior vesicular artery, marking the boundary of the surgical field.
